members.name: asin
members.extended: Alias of `arcsin`. Computes $\arcsin(x)$ for entries in $[-1, 1]$.

returns.description: Element-wise result with the same shape as `input`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> lucid.asin(x)
Tensor([...])
notes:
Mathematical definition:

$$
\text{out}_i = \arcsin(\text{input}_i), \quad \text{input}_i \in [-1, 1]
$$
Result lies in $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$. Gradient: $1 / \sqrt{1 - x^{2}}$.

members.name: acos
members.extended: Alias of `arccos`. Computes $\arccos(x)$ for entries in $[-1, 1]$.

returns.description: Element-wise result with the same shape as `input`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> lucid.acos(x)
Tensor([...])
notes:
Mathematical definition:

$$
\text{out}_i = \arccos(\text{input}_i), \quad \text{input}_i \in [-1, 1]
$$
Result lies in $[0, \pi]$. Gradient: $-1 / \sqrt{1 - x^{2}}$.

members.name: atan
members.extended: Alias of `arctan`. Returns values in $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$.

returns.description: Element-wise result with the same shape as `input`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> lucid.atan(x)
Tensor([...])
notes:
Mathematical definition:

$$
\text{out}_i = \arctan(\text{input}_i)
$$
Gradient: $1 / (1 + x^{2})$.

members.name: bitwise_not
members.extended:
Inverts every bit of each integer entry. For boolean tensors this reduces to
logical negation (`logical_not`).

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input tensor. Must have integer or boolean dtype.

returns.description: Tensor with the same shape and dtype as `input`, with every bit flipped.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([0, 1, -1], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> lucid.bitwise_not(x)
Tensor([-1, -2, 0])
notes:
For a signed two's-complement integer `x` of width $W$ bits:

$$
\text{out}_i = \,\sim x_i = -x_i - 1 \pmod{2^{W}}
$$
Not differentiable; gradients are dropped.

members.name: bitwise_and
members.extended:
Element-wise bitwise operation on integer (or boolean) operands. For boolean
inputs this reduces to the corresponding logical operation. Floating-point
inputs are not supported and will raise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Left operand. Must have integer or boolean dtype.

parameters.name: other
parameters.description: Right operand. Must have integer or boolean dtype. Broadcasts against `input`.

returns.description:
Tensor with shape `broadcast(input.shape, other.shape)` and dtype given
by the usual integer type-promotion rules.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> a = lucid.tensor([0b1100, 0b1010], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> b = lucid.tensor([0b1010, 0b0110], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> lucid.bitwise_and(a, b)
Tensor([...])
notes:
Bit-level definition (for each bit position `k` of every element):

$$
\text{out}_i[k] = \text{input}_i[k] \land \text{other}_i[k]
$$
For boolean tensors this is the same as `logical_and`. Bitwise operations are not differentiable; gradients are dropped.

members.name: bitwise_or
members.extended:
Element-wise bitwise operation on integer (or boolean) operands. For boolean
inputs this reduces to the corresponding logical operation. Floating-point
inputs are not supported and will raise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Left operand. Must have integer or boolean dtype.

parameters.name: other
parameters.description: Right operand. Must have integer or boolean dtype. Broadcasts against `input`.

returns.description:
Tensor with shape `broadcast(input.shape, other.shape)` and dtype given
by the usual integer type-promotion rules.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> a = lucid.tensor([0b1100, 0b1010], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> b = lucid.tensor([0b1010, 0b0110], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> lucid.bitwise_or(a, b)
Tensor([...])
notes:
Bit-level definition (for each bit position `k` of every element):

$$
\text{out}_i[k] = \text{input}_i[k] \lor \text{other}_i[k]
$$
For boolean tensors this is the same as `logical_or`. Bitwise operations are not differentiable; gradients are dropped.

members.name: bitwise_xor
members.extended:
Element-wise bitwise operation on integer (or boolean) operands. For boolean
inputs this reduces to the corresponding logical operation. Floating-point
inputs are not supported and will raise.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Left operand. Must have integer or boolean dtype.

parameters.name: other
parameters.description: Right operand. Must have integer or boolean dtype. Broadcasts against `input`.

returns.description:
Tensor with shape `broadcast(input.shape, other.shape)` and dtype given
by the usual integer type-promotion rules.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> a = lucid.tensor([0b1100, 0b1010], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> b = lucid.tensor([0b1010, 0b0110], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> lucid.bitwise_xor(a, b)
Tensor([...])
notes:
Bit-level definition (for each bit position `k` of every element):

$$
\text{out}_i[k] = \text{input}_i[k] \oplus \text{other}_i[k]
$$
For boolean tensors this is the same as `logical_xor`. Bitwise operations are not differentiable; gradients are dropped.

members.name: bitwise_left_shift
members.extended:
Shifts the bits of `input` to the left by `other` positions, filling
vacated low-order bits with zero. Equivalent to integer multiplication by
$2^{\text{other}}$ modulo the dtype width.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Left operand. Must have integer dtype.

parameters.name: other
parameters.description:
Number of positions to shift. Must be a non-negative integer; shifting by
more than the bit-width of `input` is undefined and clamped or wrapped
depending on the underlying integer dtype.

returns.description: Shifted tensor with shape `broadcast(input.shape, other.shape)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> a = lucid.tensor([1, 2, 4], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> lucid.bitwise_left_shift(a, 2)
Tensor([4, 8, 16])
notes:
Mathematical definition (modulo the dtype's bit width $W$):

$$
\text{out}_i = (\text{input}_i \cdot 2^{\text{other}_i}) \bmod 2^{W}
$$
High-order bits are lost on overflow.  Not differentiable.

members.name: bitwise_right_shift
members.extended:
Shifts the bits of `input` to the right by `other` positions. For signed
integer dtypes the shift is arithmetic (sign-extending); for unsigned dtypes
it is logical (zero-filling).

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Left operand. Must have integer dtype.

parameters.name: other
parameters.description: Number of positions to shift. Must be a non-negative integer.

returns.description: Shifted tensor with shape `broadcast(input.shape, other.shape)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> a = lucid.tensor([8, 16, 32], dtype=lucid.int32)
>>> lucid.bitwise_right_shift(a, 2)
Tensor([2, 4, 8])
notes:
Mathematical definition (signed, with floor division):

$$
\text{out}_i = \lfloor \text{input}_i / 2^{\text{other}_i} \rfloor
$$
Shifting by an amount greater than or equal to the dtype's bit width is
undefined behaviour and should be avoided.  Not differentiable.

members.name: masked_select
members.summary: Return a 1-D tensor of elements where `mask` is True.
members.extended:
The mask must be broadcast-compatible with `input`.  The output is
always rank 1 regardless of input rank — ordering follows row-major
traversal.

returns.description: 1-D tensor of length `mask.sum().item()`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([[1, 2], [3, 4]])
>>> m = x > 2
>>> lucid.masked_select(x, m)
Tensor([3, 4])
notes:
Because the output size is data-dependent this operation may force
a host round-trip on accelerator backends; prefer `where` when
a fixed-shape result is acceptable.

members.name: isclose
members.signature: isclose(input: Tensor, other: Tensor, rtol: float = ..., atol: float = ..., equal_nan: bool = ...)
members.summary: Element-wise approximate equality with absolute and relative tolerance.
members.extended:
Returns a boolean tensor that is `True` where the two operands are equal up
to a combined absolute / relative tolerance. Useful for comparing
floating-point results where bit-exact equality is too strict.

parameters.name: equal_nan
parameters.description: If `True`, two NaN entries compare equal.

returns.description: Boolean tensor (dtype `bool`) with shape `broadcast(input.shape, other.shape)`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> a = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0])
>>> b = lucid.tensor([1.0 + 1e-9, 2.5])
>>> lucid.isclose(a, b, rtol=1e-5, atol=1e-8)
Tensor([True, False])
notes:
Mathematical definition:

$$
\text{out}_i = \bigl( |x_i - y_i| \le \text{atol} + \text{rtol} \cdot |y_i| \bigr)
$$
When `equal_nan` is `True` positions where both operands are NaN are
additionally marked `True`. Not differentiable.

members.name: view
members.summary: Return a view of `input` with a different shape.
members.extended:
The total number of elements must be preserved.  Unlike
`reshape`, `view` *requires* the input to be contiguous
over the affected dims and never copies — if a view is not possible
a runtime error is raised.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Source tensor.  Must be contiguous w.r.t. the requested layout.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Target shape.  Exactly one entry may be `-1` (auto-inferred).

returns.description: Tensor sharing storage with `input`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.arange(12)
>>> lucid.view(x, (3, 4)).shape
(3, 4)
notes:
Use `view` when zero-copy is part of the contract; use
`reshape` when correctness should take precedence over
layout assumptions.

members.name: concat
members.extended:
Concatenates a list of tensors along an existing dimension.  Provided
for naming parity with array libraries that prefer the longer name.

returns.description: The concatenated tensor.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> lucid.concat([lucid.tensor([1, 2]), lucid.tensor([3, 4])], dim=0)
Tensor([1, 2, 3, 4])
notes:
Behaviour is identical to `cat` — see that function for the
precise contract and edge cases.

members.name: real
members.summary: Real part of a complex tensor.
members.extended: For complex inputs, returns the real component as a real-valued tensor of the same shape; for real inputs it returns the input unchanged.

returns.description: Element-wise result with the same shape as `input`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> lucid.real(x)
Tensor([...])
notes:
Mathematical definition:

$$
\text{out}_i = \mathrm{Re}(\text{input}_i)
$$
Gradient flows back as the real part of the upstream cotangent.

members.name: imag
members.summary: Imaginary part of a complex tensor.
members.extended: For complex inputs, returns the imaginary component as a real-valued tensor of the same shape; for real inputs it returns a zero tensor.

returns.description: Element-wise result with the same shape as `input`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> lucid.imag(x)
Tensor([...])
notes:
Mathematical definition:

$$
\text{out}_i = \mathrm{Im}(\text{input}_i)
$$
Gradient flows back as the imaginary part of the upstream cotangent.

members.name: conj
members.extended: Negates the imaginary part of each entry. For real inputs it is a no-op (returns the input).

returns.description: Element-wise result with the same shape as `input`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
>>> lucid.conj(x)
Tensor([...])
notes:
Mathematical definition:

$$
\text{out}_i = \overline{\text{input}_i} = \mathrm{Re}(x_i) - i\,\mathrm{Im}(x_i)
$$
Involution: `conj(conj(x)) == x`. Used in inner products on complex vector spaces.

members.name: sum
members.signature: sum(x: Tensor, dim: _int | list[_int] | None = None, keepdim: _bool = False, correction: _int = 1)

members.name: mean
members.signature: mean(x: Tensor, dim: _int | list[_int] | None = None, keepdim: _bool = False)
members.extended:
The sample mean of `n` values is

$$
\bar{x} \;=\; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i .
$$
Floating-point results are accumulated in the same dtype as the input;
integer inputs are promoted to the default floating dtype before the
reduction is performed.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Dimension(s) to reduce.  `None` reduces over all elements.

parameters.name: keepdim
parameters.description: Whether to retain the reduced dim(s) with size 1.

returns.description: Reduced tensor of floating dtype.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 2.0], [3.0, 4.0]])
>>> lucid.mean(x)
Tensor(2.5)
>>> lucid.mean(x, dim=0)
Tensor([2., 3.])
notes:
Mean is an unbiased estimator of the population mean.  For empty
reductions (size-0 `dim`) the result is `nan`.

members.name: prod
members.signature: prod(x: Tensor, dim: _int | list[_int] | None = None, keepdim: _bool = False)
members.summary: Compute the product of elements along `dim`.
members.extended:
The multiplicative analogue of `sum`.  Reducing along `dim`
produces a tensor whose entries equal the product of all values that
were collapsed.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Dimension(s) to reduce.  `None` reduces over the whole tensor.

returns.description: Reduced tensor.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0])
>>> lucid.prod(x)
Tensor(24.)
notes:
$$
y \;=\; \prod_{i} x_i .
$$
For numerical reasons, products of many small values are better
computed via `exp(sum(log(x)))`.

members.name: max
members.signature: max(x: Tensor, dim: _int | list[_int] | None = None, keepdim: _bool = False)

members.name: min
members.signature: min(x: Tensor, dim: _int | list[_int] | None = None, keepdim: _bool = False)

members.name: var
members.signature: var(x: Tensor, dim: _int | list[_int] | None = None, keepdim: _bool = False, correction: _int = 1)

returns.description: Reduced floating-point tensor.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0])
>>> lucid.var(x)
Tensor(1.6667)
notes:
$$
\mathrm{Var}(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{n - c}
    \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2 ,
$$
with $c \in \{0, 1\}$ set by the `correction` keyword.

members.name: std
members.signature: std(x: Tensor, dim: _int | list[_int] | None = None, keepdim: _bool = False, correction: _int = 1)
members.summary: Compute the sample standard deviation along `dim`.
members.extended:
Defined as the square root of the (corrected) sample variance.  The
`correction` keyword selects between Bessel-corrected (`correction=1`,
the default — divides by $n-1$) and biased (`correction=0` —
divides by $n$) estimates.

parameters.name: keepdim
parameters.description: Whether to retain reduced dims with size 1.

parameters.name: correction
parameters.description: Degrees-of-freedom correction.  `1` for unbiased, `0` for biased.

returns.description: Reduced floating-point tensor.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0])
>>> lucid.std(x)
Tensor(1.2910)
notes:
$$
\sigma \;=\; \sqrt{ \frac{1}{n - c}
    \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2 } ,
$$
where $c$ is the correction and $\bar{x}$ is the sample
mean.

members.name: argmax
members.signature: argmax(x: Tensor, dim: _int | None = None, keepdim: _bool = False)
members.summary: Return the indices of the maximum values along `dim`.
members.extended:
For each slice along `dim` the index of the largest element is returned.
When `dim` is `None` the tensor is treated as flattened and a single
scalar index into the flattened layout is produced.  Ties are broken by
returning the *first* occurrence in row-major order.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description:
Dimension along which to reduce.  `None` means reduce over the
whole tensor.

parameters.name: keepdim
parameters.description: Retain the reduced dimension with size 1 when `True`.

returns.description:
`int64` tensor of indices.  Shape matches `x.shape` with `dim`
removed (or set to 1 when `keepdim=True`).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([[1.0, 5.0, 2.0], [4.0, 0.0, 3.0]])
>>> lucid.argmax(x, dim=1)
Tensor([1, 0])
notes:
Mathematically, for a 1-D input $x$,

$$
\mathrm{argmax}(x) = \underset{i}{\arg\max}\; x_i.
$$

members.name: argmin
members.signature: argmin(x: Tensor, dim: _int | None = None, keepdim: _bool = False)
members.summary: Return the indices of the minimum values along `dim`.
members.extended:
Symmetric to `argmax` — for each slice along `dim` the index of
the smallest element is produced, with ties broken by returning the first
occurrence in row-major order.  `dim=None` reduces over the flattened
tensor.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Reduction dimension.  `None` reduces over all elements.

parameters.name: keepdim
parameters.description: Whether to keep the reduced dim with size 1.

returns.description: `int64` index tensor.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([3.0, 1.0, 4.0, 1.0])
>>> lucid.argmin(x)
Tensor(1)
notes:
$$
\mathrm{argmin}(x) = \underset{i}{\arg\min}\; x_i.
$$

members.name: squeeze
members.signature: squeeze(x: Tensor, dim: _int | list[_int] | None = None)
members.extended:
When `dim` is `None` *every* size-1 axis is removed.  When `dim`
is given, only that axis is removed and only if its size is 1
(otherwise the input is returned unchanged).

returns.description: View into `x` with the chosen size-1 axes removed.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.zeros(1, 3, 1, 4)
>>> lucid.squeeze(x).shape
(3, 4)
notes:
Squeeze is the inverse of `unsqueeze`.  Be cautious in generic
code: a "safe" `squeeze` for the last axis is
`x.squeeze(-1) if x.size(-1) == 1 else x`.

members.name: repeat
members.signature: repeat(x: Tensor, repeats: _int, dim: _int | None = None)
members.summary: Tile a tensor along an existing dimension.
members.extended:
Replicates `input` as a whole block `repeats` times along `dim`.
Contrast with `repeat_interleave`, which replicates each element
individually (`[1, 1, 2, 2]` vs. `[1, 2, 1, 2]`).

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Dimension along which to repeat.  `None` flattens first.

returns.description: Newly-allocated tensor — repeating always copies.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1, 2, 3])
>>> lucid.repeat(x, 2, dim=0)
Tensor([1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3])
notes:
For multi-dim tiling patterns use `tile`, which accepts a
per-axis repetition vector.

members.name: repeat_interleave
members.signature: repeat_interleave(x: Tensor, repeats: _int, dim: _int | None = None)
members.summary: Repeat each element of `x` `repeats` times along `dim`.
members.extended:
The opposite ordering of `repeat`: instead of replicating the
whole block, each individual element (or sub-slice) is replicated
contiguously before moving on.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Axis along which to interleave.  `None` flattens first.

returns.description:
Newly-allocated tensor with size `repeats * x.size(dim)` along
`dim`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1, 2, 3])
>>> lucid.repeat_interleave(x, 2, dim=0)
Tensor([1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3])
notes:
Useful for upsampling tensors by integer factors without
interpolation (nearest-neighbour upsample = interleave + reshape).

members.name: split
members.summary: Split a tensor into pieces of explicit sizes along `dim`.
members.extended:
If `split_size_or_sections` is an integer, the tensor is split into
equal pieces of that size (except possibly the last, which may be
smaller).  If it is a list, each entry specifies the size of one
output piece and the list must sum to `input.size(dim)`.

parameters.name: split_size_or_sections
parameters.description: Per-piece size, or a list of sizes.

returns.description: Views into `x` — no data is copied.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.arange(7)
>>> [t.shape for t in lucid.split(x, [2, 3, 2])]
[(2,), (3,), (2,)]
notes:
For "as many pieces as fit" semantics with no remainder constraint,
prefer `chunk`.

members.name: tensordot
members.signature: tensordot(a: Tensor, b: Tensor, dims: _int | list[list[_int]] = 2)
members.summary: Generalized tensor contraction over arbitrary axes.
members.extended:
Contracts `a` and `b` along the axes specified by `dims`, generalizing
matrix multiplication to higher-rank tensors. `dims` may be:

* an `int` `N` — contract the last `N` axes of `a` with the first
  `N` axes of `b`;
* a pair of axis lists `[[a_axes], [b_axes]]` — contract the listed axes of
  `a` against the listed axes of `b` pairwise.

parameters.name: dims
parameters.annotation: int or list of list of int
parameters.description: Specification of which axes to contract. Default is `2`.

returns.description:
Contracted result whose remaining axes are the un-contracted axes of `a`
followed by those of `b`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> a = lucid.zeros((3, 4, 5))
>>> b = lucid.zeros((4, 5, 2))
>>> lucid.tensordot(a, b, dims=2).shape
(3, 2)
notes:
Mathematical definition for axis lists $I_a, I_b$:

$$
\text{out}_{\bar I_a,\, \bar I_b} = \sum_{I_a = I_b} a_{\dots}\, b_{\dots}
$$
where $\bar I$ denotes the axes not contracted. Equivalent to a suitable
permutation followed by `matmul` and a reshape.

members.name: meshgrid
members.summary: Construct coordinate grids from 1-D coordinate vectors.
members.extended:
Given `k` 1-D inputs of sizes `n_1, ..., n_k`, returns `k`
tensors of shape `(n_1, ..., n_k)` (`'ij'` indexing) or
`(n_2, n_1, n_3, ..., n_k)` (`'xy'` indexing) such that
`out[i][...]` enumerates the `i`-th coordinate.

parameters.name: indexing
parameters.description:
`'ij'` for matrix-style indexing, `'xy'` for Cartesian
indexing (swaps the first two output axes).

returns.description: `len(tensors)` coordinate grids.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1, 2, 3])
>>> y = lucid.tensor([4, 5])
>>> gx, gy = lucid.meshgrid(x, y, indexing='ij')
>>> gx.shape, gy.shape
((3, 2), (3, 2))
notes:
The `'xy'` convention matches NumPy's default and is convenient
for plotting; `'ij'` matches the broadcasting/reduction
conventions used elsewhere in tensor libraries.

members.name: diff
members.summary: Compute the `n`-th order finite difference along an axis.
members.extended:
The first-order difference is $y_i = x_{i+1} - x_i$; higher
orders apply the operation recursively.  Each application reduces
the size of the chosen axis by one.

returns.description: Tensor with the differenced axis shorter by `n` elements.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> x = lucid.tensor([1.0, 3.0, 6.0, 10.0])
>>> lucid.diff(x)
Tensor([2., 3., 4.])
notes:
$$
\Delta^n x[i] \;=\; \Delta^{n-1} x[i+1] \;-\; \Delta^{n-1} x[i] ,
\qquad \Delta^0 x = x .
$$
First-order differences are widely used as a discrete approximation
to the derivative on a unit-spaced grid.

members.name: lerp
members.summary: Linearly interpolate between `input` and an endpoint with a weight.
members.extended:
Computes the affine combination

$$
\text{out} \;=\; x + w \cdot (\text{end} - x)
\;=\; (1 - w) \cdot x + w \cdot \text{end} ,
$$
where $w$ is the interpolation weight.  `weight` may be a
scalar or a tensor broadcastable to `input`.

returns.description: Interpolated tensor of the broadcast shape.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> a = lucid.tensor([0.0, 0.0, 0.0])
>>> b = lucid.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 4.0])
>>> lucid.lerp(a, b, 0.5)
Tensor([0.5, 1. , 2. ])
notes:
With `w = 0` the result equals `input`; with `w = 1` it equals
`end`; out-of-range weights extrapolate beyond the endpoints.
